blocks.title: CALORIES Trial — Enteral vs Parenteral Nutrition in ICU
blocks.population: 2400 adult ICU patients — unable to receive oral nutrition within 24h of admission (UK multicentre RCT)
blocks.intervention: Early parenteral nutrition (PN) within 36h of ICU admission
blocks.control: Early enteral nutrition (EN) within 36h of ICU admission
blocks.results: EN vs PN: no difference in 30-day mortality (33.1% vs 33.8%; p=0.57). EN: fewer hypoglycaemic events (3.7% vs 6.2%; p<0.001) and fewer vomiting episodes. PN: higher caloric delivery. No significant difference in infectious complications, LOS, or 90-day mortality.
blocks.conclusion: In unselected ICU patients, early EN and early PN have equivalent mortality. EN preferred due to fewer metabolic complications (hypoglycaemia) and lower cost. Use EN when gut is functional; reserve PN for non-functioning gut.
blocks.board_relevance: CALORIES trial: EN = PN for ICU mortality when both started early. EN preferred (fewer complications, cheaper). Do NOT delay nutrition waiting for gut to 'fully function' — start EN early if safe.

blocks.title: Glutamine in ICU — Evidence Reversal (ESPEN 2023 Confirmed)
blocks.content: Glutamine was the archetypal immunonutrient: primary fuel for enterocytes and immune cells; conditionally essential in critical illness (endogenous synthesis insufficient). Early RCTs showed benefit. However: REDOXS trial (NEJM 2013 — Heyland): high-dose enteral + parenteral glutamine (0.35 g/kg/day IV) in critically ill patients with multi-organ failure INCREASED 28-day mortality (32.4% vs 27.2%; p=0.05). METAPLUS trial: similar findings. **ESPEN 2023 position (confirmed)**: glutamine should NOT be given routinely to critically ill patients with organ failure or haemodynamic instability. Parenteral glutamine 0.2–0.4 g/kg/day may be appropriate in stable ICU patients on PN without organ failure. **ESPEN 2023 key updates for surgical patients:** (1) **Immunonutrition** (arginine + omega-3 + RNA): Grade A for malnourished patients undergoing major GI cancer surgery (5–7 days preoperatively + postoperative); Grade B for well-nourished patients undergoing major upper GI surgery; NOT recommended for well-nourished colorectal surgery patients. (2) **NRS-2002 screening** is mandatory for ALL surgical patients at admission — NRS-2002 ≥3 triggers mandatory nutritional assessment and may justify delaying elective surgery for 7–14 days of nutritional optimisation (Grade A). (3) **IV omega-3 enriched lipid emulsions** (fish oil-based) preferred over pure soybean oil when parenteral nutrition required perioperatively (Grade A — reduced infectious complications).

questions.question: A 70-year-old man admitted for elective colonic resection has lost 8% of his body weight in the past 3 months and is eating approximately 50% of his normal food intake. He has no severe systemic illness. He is 71 years old. What is his NRS-2002 score and what action should be taken?
options: A. NRS-2002 score 2 — low risk; weekly rescreening only
B. NRS-2002 score 3 — at nutritional risk; initiate nutritional support plan
C. NRS-2002 score 4 — high risk; urgent parenteral nutrition indicated
D. NRS-2002 score 1 — low risk; standard surgical preparation
E. NRS-2002 score 5 — critical; postpone surgery pending nutritional correction
questions.explanation: NRS-2002 scoring: Nutritional status: weight loss 8% in 3 months (>5% in 3 months) + food intake 50% of normal = score 2. Disease severity: major abdominal surgery (colonic resection) = score 2. Age ≥70 = +1. Total: 2 + 2 + 1 = 5? Actually, the disease severity score reflects the CURRENT illness severity (the reason for scoring now, before surgery). If scored pre-operatively with the patient not yet having surgery: the planned surgery is 'high risk' so disease severity = 3 (major abdominal). Nutritional status: weight loss >5% in 3 months AND food intake 50–75% = nutritional score 2. Age 71 = +1. Total = 2 + 2 + 1 = 5. However, interpreting conservatively: nutritional score 2 + disease severity 2 + age 1 = 5. NRS ≥3 = at nutritional risk → initiate nutritional support. Regardless of the exact score, this patient has NRS ≥3 and requires nutritional intervention. The most precise answer is B (at risk → initiate nutritional support plan). Note: for pre-operative scoring, 'major surgery planned' contributes to the disease severity component.
questions.teaching_point: NRS-2002 ≥3 = nutritional risk → nutritional support plan. Key components: nutritional status (weight loss, food intake, BMI) + disease severity (metabolic stress) + age ≥70 (+1). Surgical patients: major abdominal surgery = disease severity score 2–3. Always include age adjustment. Pre-operative NRS ≥3 → oral nutritional supplements (ONS) for ≥5 days pre-op (ESPEN recommendation).

questions.question: A 58-year-old woman is on Day 4 post-oesophagectomy. Her albumin is 22 g/L (pre-operative albumin was 34 g/L). The ward team considers giving IV albumin infusion to correct the low level. What is the most appropriate action?
options: A. Administer 100 mL 20% albumin IV — correct the albumin deficit
B. Do not give albumin infusion — low albumin reflects surgical inflammatory response; ensure adequate EN via jejunostomy and protein supplementation
C. Start parenteral nutrition immediately — low albumin indicates inadequate EN
D. Investigate for protein-losing enteropathy — this is an abnormal post-operative albumin fall
E. Restrict protein intake — excess protein overwhelms hepatic synthesis capacity
questions.explanation: Post-operative hypoalbuminaemia (albumin 22 g/L on Day 4 post-oesophagectomy) is EXPECTED and NORMAL — it reflects the acute surgical inflammatory response, not malnutrition. Mechanisms: (1) Redistribution to extravascular space via increased capillary permeability (capillary leak); (2) Reduced hepatic albumin synthesis (liver prioritises acute-phase proteins — CRP, fibrinogen, complement — over albumin); (3) Dilutional effect from resuscitation fluids. IV albumin infusion to correct low albumin has NO evidence of benefit in surgical patients and is expensive (SAFE trial — 0.9% saline equivalent to 4% albumin for ICU resuscitation). The correct action: ensure adequate EN via jejunostomy (protein target 1.5–2.0 g/kg/day), monitor C-reactive protein (CRP falling = resolving inflammation = albumin will recover), continue ERAS protocol. Albumin will normalise as inflammation resolves.
questions.teaching_point: Post-op albumin fall is NORMAL and expected — reflects inflammatory response (IL-6 → hepatic acute phase response → ↓albumin synthesis + ↑capillary leak). Do NOT give IV albumin to correct low albumin. IV albumin indications: spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP), large-volume paracentesis (>5L), hepatorenal syndrome. Nutritional management: provide adequate dietary protein (1.5–2.0 g/kg/day) — albumin will recover as inflammation resolves.

questions.question: A 65-year-old man is in the surgical ICU after emergency Hartmann's procedure for perforated sigmoid diverticulitis. He is non-diabetic but his blood glucose on admission is 14.2 mmol/L. An insulin infusion is started. What is the target blood glucose range according to current NICE-SUGAR evidence?
options: A. 4.0–6.0 mmol/L — tight glycaemic control to minimise hyperglycaemia
B. 6.0–10.0 mmol/L — moderate glycaemic control
C. 8.0–12.0 mmol/L — permissive hyperglycaemia to avoid hypoglycaemia
D. <8.0 mmol/L — as per Van den Berghe original ICU trial
E. >10 mmol/L — only treat if glucose >15 mmol/L
questions.explanation: The NICE-SUGAR trial (NEJM 2009) demonstrated that intensive insulin therapy targeting 4.5–6.0 mmol/L INCREASED 90-day mortality compared to conventional management (target ≤10 mmol/L). The higher mortality in the intensive arm was attributed largely to severe hypoglycaemic events (≤2.2 mmol/L: 6.8% intensive vs 0.5% conventional). Current international guidelines (ESPEN, ASPEN, SCCM): target blood glucose 6–10 mmol/L in critically ill patients. This represents a balance: avoiding hyperglycaemia (immune dysfunction, osmotic diuresis, wound healing impairment) while preventing hypoglycaemia (cerebral injury, cardiovascular events). Check glucose every 1–2 hours with insulin infusion; adjust infusion rate to maintain target. Use hospital insulin infusion protocol.
questions.teaching_point: NICE-SUGAR: glucose target 6–10 mmol/L in ICU. NOT tight control (4.5–6) — increases mortality. Hypoglycaemia <4 mmol/L = immediately dangerous. In surgical ICU: monitor 1–2 hourly; use validated insulin infusion protocol. In ward surgical patients: glucose <11 mmol/L is the general target; tighter control in ICU.

questions.question: A 55-year-old man is admitted to the surgical ICU following a laparotomy for small bowel volvulus. He has an anastomosis but the bowel appeared viable. He is intubated and haemodynamically stable on noradrenaline 0.1 µg/kg/min. His abdomen is tender but there is no ileus on AXR. According to the CALORIES trial, what is the preferred nutritional strategy?
options: A. Parenteral nutrition — gut is unsafe to use while patient is on vasopressors
B. Enteral nutrition via NGT within 24–48 hours — preferred over PN if gut is functional
C. Delay all nutrition for 5 days — early nutrition worsens anastomotic healing
D. Peripheral parenteral nutrition — safest option with a central catheter already in situ
E. Oral clear fluids only — PN is associated with higher CLABSI rates
questions.explanation: The CALORIES trial (Harvey, NEJM 2014) demonstrated equivalent 30-day mortality between early EN and early PN in ICU patients. EN had fewer hypoglycaemic episodes and lower cost. The principle: 'if the gut works, use it.' This patient has a patent gut with viable bowel and no active obstruction — EN via NGT is appropriate. Vasopressors are NOT an absolute contraindication to EN: current SCCM/ASPEN 2022 guidelines support initiating trophic EN (10–20 mL/h) even in haemodynamically compromised patients on low-moderate vasopressors, advancing as vasopressors are weaned. EN should be withheld if vasopressors are at high doses (MAP <50 with maximum vasopressor support — high risk of non-occlusive mesenteric ischaemia). Low-dose noradrenaline (0.1 µg/kg/min) does not preclude EN. Start at low rate (20 mL/h) and monitor for tolerance.
questions.teaching_point: CALORIES trial: EN = PN for ICU mortality; EN preferred (fewer complications, cheaper). EN safe with low-moderate vasopressors (start trophic 10–20 mL/h). Hold EN if: haemodynamic collapse (MAP <50 on maximum support), bowel ischaemia suspected, uncontrolled GI haemorrhage, intestinal obstruction. Start EN within 24–48h in ICU after haemodynamic stabilisation.

questions.question: A 42-year-old anorexic woman (BMI 13.5, no nutritional intake for 14 days) is commenced on enteral nutrition via NGT at 500 mL/day. 36 hours later, her serum phosphate is 0.35 mmol/L (was 0.8 mmol/L before feeding), potassium 2.8 mmol/L, and she is confused and weak. What is the mechanism of this phosphate drop?
options: A. Phosphate is being lost in the urine from osmotic diuresis
B. Insulin release driven by carbohydrate refeeding shifts phosphate intracellularly, dropping serum levels
C. Malnutrition causes phosphate malabsorption from the gut
D. EN formula lacks sufficient phosphate — switch to a high-phosphate feed
E. Renal failure is causing phosphate wasting
questions.explanation: Refeeding syndrome pathophysiology: during prolonged starvation, intracellular phosphate is depleted (used for cellular energy metabolism) while serum phosphate is maintained by regulatory mechanisms (bone phosphate release, PTH regulation). When carbohydrates are re-introduced: (1) Blood glucose rises → pancreas releases INSULIN → (2) Insulin drives cellular uptake of glucose, phosphate, potassium, and magnesium simultaneously → (3) Serum phosphate drops rapidly ('phosphate shift intracellularly'). The total body phosphate is already depleted, so even a small additional intracellular shift causes severe hypophosphataemia. Clinical consequences: severe hypophosphataemia (0.35 mmol/L = severe: <0.5 mmol/L) → ATP depletion in all cells → muscle weakness (including diaphragm), red cell haemolysis, platelet dysfunction, cardiac arrhythmias, Wernicke's encephalopathy if thiamine-deficient. This patient was NOT given thiamine before feeding (a critical error in this scenario).
questions.teaching_point: Refeeding syndrome mechanism: starvation depletes intracellular phosphate → carbohydrate reintroduction → insulin surge → phosphate + K+ + Mg2+ shift intracellularly → severe hypo-phosphataemia (<0.5 mmol/L). Key: give thiamine (Pabrinex IV) BEFORE any carbohydrate. Start feeding at 5–10 kcal/kg/day. Monitor phosphate, K+, Mg2+ daily. Correct electrolytes before restarting nutrition.

questions.question: Which of the following patients is at HIGHEST risk of refeeding syndrome according to NICE CG32 major risk criteria?
options: A. A 60-year-old with BMI 19, eating 70% of diet for 2 weeks
B. A 45-year-old alcohol-dependent man admitted with neglect — has eaten minimally for 12 days; BMI 15.5; serum phosphate 0.55 mmol/L
C. A 55-year-old with BMI 23 on chemotherapy, eating 50% normal intake for 5 days
D. A 70-year-old with controlled Type 2 diabetes, BMI 28, eating normally
E. A 35-year-old post-appendicectomy, NBM for 3 days
questions.explanation: NICE CG32 major risk factors for refeeding syndrome: BMI <16 kg/m², unintentional weight loss >15% in 3–6 months, negligible nutritional intake >10 days, low levels of K+/phosphate/Mg2+ BEFORE STARTING feeding. Patient B has: BMI 15.5 (<16 = 1 major criterion), minimal intake for 12 days (>10 days = 1 major criterion), alcohol dependence (minor factor), and serum phosphate 0.55 mmol/L before feeding (borderline — below 0.6 approaches the 'low levels before feeding' criterion). This patient meets 2+ major criteria → highest risk. Patient A (BMI 19, 70% intake, 2 weeks) meets no major criteria. Patient C (BMI 23, 50% intake for 5 days) has 1 minor criterion. Patient E (3 days NBM) — insufficient duration to reach at-risk threshold.
questions.teaching_point: NICE CG32 refeeding at-risk: meet 1+ MAJOR criterion OR 2+ minor criteria. Major: BMI <16, weight loss >15%/3–6 months, negligible intake >10 days, low electrolytes pre-feeding. Minor: BMI 16–18.5, weight loss 10–15%, intake negligible >5 days, alcohol excess, drugs (diuretics, insulin, antacids). BMI <16 = immediate flag for refeeding protocol.

questions.question: A 68-year-old man with pharyngeal carcinoma is referred for PEG placement to enable long-term enteral nutrition. His INR is 1.8 (on warfarin for AF). CT abdomen shows the colon overlying the anterior stomach. What is the most appropriate management?
options: A. Proceed with PEG — colon overlying the stomach is incidental
B. Withhold warfarin for 5 days until INR <1.5, then insert PEG as planned
C. PEG is contraindicated by both INR >1.5 and colonic interposition — plan for surgical jejunostomy or radiologically inserted gastrostomy (RIG)
D. Continue PEG insertion after correcting INR — colonic interposition can be managed by ultrasound guidance
E. Long-term NGT is equivalent to PEG — avoid all percutaneous approaches
questions.explanation: This patient has TWO contraindications to standard endoscopic PEG insertion: (1) INR 1.8 (>1.5) — coagulopathy contraindication; risk of significant bleeding at puncture site; must correct INR to <1.5 before PEG. (2) Colonic interposition — colon lying between the anterior abdominal wall and stomach on CT means the endoscope cannot safely transilluminate a 'safe window'; if a PEG needle is inserted, it may traverse the colon → colocutaneous fistula (severe complication). Management options: (a) Radiologically inserted gastrostomy (RIG) — real-time fluoroscopic guidance allows identification and avoidance of colonic interposition, with air insufflation under fluoroscopy; INR correction still required. (b) Surgical jejunostomy — long-term feeding access placed under direct vision; no transillumination needed; can be inserted despite colonic anatomy; preferred if colonic interposition cannot be managed by RIG. Option B (correct INR and proceed) ignores the colonic interposition contraindication.
questions.teaching_point: PEG contraindications: INR >1.5, Plt <50 (coagulopathy); inability to transilluminate (obesity, colonic interposition, prior gastric surgery, liver enlargement); ascites; peritoneal dialysis; bowel obstruction preventing endoscope passage. Alternative if PEG not feasible: RIG (radiologically inserted gastrostomy) or surgical jejunostomy. INR and platelet correction required before any percutaneous procedure.

questions.question: A surgical ward introduces a CLABSI prevention bundle for patients receiving parenteral nutrition. Which single intervention from the bundle has the highest level of evidence for reducing central line infection rates?
options: A. Changing IV tubing every 24 hours
B. Using antimicrobial-impregnated catheters for all PN lines
C. Chlorhexidine-based skin antisepsis before CVC insertion combined with maximal sterile barrier precautions
D. Prophylactic systemic antibiotics during CVC insertion
E. Using femoral site for CVC insertion to allow supine patient positioning
questions.explanation: Multiple RCTs and systematic reviews demonstrate that chlorhexidine-based skin antisepsis (2% chlorhexidine-alcohol — e.g., ChloraPrep) combined with maximal sterile barrier precautions (full gown, gloves, mask, cap, full-body sterile drape) during CVC insertion constitute the highest-evidence interventions in CLABSI prevention bundles. These two measures, along with hand hygiene, are the core of the SHEA/IDSA CLABSI prevention guidelines (Level IA — strongest evidence). Chlorhexidine impregnated dressings are also evidence-based (Level II). Antimicrobial catheters (Option B) are recommended only as a supplemental measure when bundle compliance is high and CLABSI rate remains above target — not a first-line standard. Prophylactic antibiotics (Option D) are NOT recommended (resistance risk, no consistent benefit for CLABSI). Femoral site (Option E) has the HIGHEST infection rate — should be avoided. Tubing change every 96 hours (not 24h) unless blood products or lipid-containing PN (change every 24h for those).
questions.teaching_point: CLABSI prevention — highest evidence interventions: (1) Hand hygiene; (2) Chlorhexidine skin antisepsis (2% CHG-alcohol); (3) Maximal sterile barrier precautions; (4) Subclavian preferred site (lowest CLABSI); (5) Daily line review — remove when unnecessary. Bundle compliance, not individual elements, reduces CLABSI. Target <1 per 1000 catheter-days.

questions.question: A 35-year-old woman with short bowel syndrome following mesenteric ischaemia has been on home parenteral nutrition for 3 years. Her LFTs show progressive worsening: ALT 4× ULN, ALP 3× ULN, bilirubin 48 µmol/L. Liver biopsy shows cholestasis and early fibrosis. What is the most appropriate intervention?
options: A. Switch to 0.9% saline — the PN is causing hepatotoxicity through electrolyte content
B. Switch lipid emulsion to fish oil-enriched IVFE (SMOFlipid); cycle PN 16–18h/day; maximise any residual enteral intake; consider ursodeoxycholic acid
C. Stop all PN immediately — the liver cannot tolerate further PN
D. Reduce amino acid content to 0.5 g/kg/day — protein overload causes hepatotoxicity
E. Prescribe rifaximin to treat small bowel bacterial overgrowth only
questions.explanation: PN-associated liver disease (PNALD/IFALD) is managed with a multi-pronged approach: (1) Switch soybean oil-based IVFE to fish oil-enriched IVFE (SMOFlipid or Omegaven) — fish oil reduces phytosterol load (soybean phytosterols impair bile flow), provides omega-3 fatty acids (anti-inflammatory), and reduces hepatotoxic phytosterol accumulation. RCT evidence: Omegaven (fish oil) reversed PNALD in paediatric patients and shows benefit in adults. (2) Cycling PN to 16–18h/day allows 6–8h of lipolysis and hepatic lipid clearance (prevents steatosis). (3) Maximise any enteral intake — even small volumes stimulate bile flow (CCK-mediated), prevent SBBO, and reduce PN dependence. (4) Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) — hepato-protective, improves bile flow. (5) Treat small bowel bacterial overgrowth (SBBO) with rotating antibiotics (rifaximin, metronidazole). Stopping PN entirely (Option C) is not possible in type III intestinal failure patients who cannot maintain nutrition by any oral/enteral route. Reducing amino acids (Option D) would worsen protein malnutrition.
questions.teaching_point: PNALD/IFALD management: (1) Switch to SMOFlipid/Omegaven (fish oil IVFE); (2) Cycle PN 16–18h; (3) Maximise EN even small volumes; (4) UDCA; (5) Treat SBBO. End-stage IFALD: combined intestinal-liver transplant is the only curative option in type III intestinal failure. SMOFlipid is now standard for long-term PN in most UK centres.

questions.question: A 62-year-old malnourished man (NRS-2002 score 5, weight loss 12% in 2 months) undergoes elective oesophagectomy for squamous cell carcinoma. According to ESPEN 2021 guidelines on immunonutrition, what is the recommended pre-operative nutritional strategy?
options: A. Standard polymeric EN via NGT for 7 days preoperatively
B. Oral immunonutrition (arginine + omega-3 + nucleotides — e.g., IMPACT) for 5–7 days preoperatively in malnourished patients
C. Parenteral nutrition for 10 days preoperatively — gut absorption is unreliable in oesophageal cancer
D. No specific nutritional preparation — proceed to surgery
E. High-dose glutamine supplementation 0.35 g/kg/day IV for 7 days preoperatively
questions.explanation: ESPEN 2021 Clinical Nutrition in Surgery guidelines: for malnourished patients (NRS-2002 ≥3) undergoing major upper GI surgery (including oesophagectomy), PRE-OPERATIVE immunonutrition with oral formulas containing arginine + omega-3 fatty acids + nucleotides (e.g., IMPACT, Oral Impact — Nestlé) for 5–7 days pre-operatively is recommended (Grade B, Level II). This improves immune function, reduces post-operative infectious complications, and may reduce hospital stay. The recommendation is specifically for malnourished patients — non-malnourished patients do not demonstrate clear benefit from immunonutrition. High-dose glutamine (Option E) is no longer recommended in ICU (REDOXS trial showed harm in organ failure) and is not the immunonutrient of first choice. Standard EN (Option A) is appropriate for nutritional support but lacks the specific immune modulation of immunonutrition.
questions.teaching_point: ESPEN immunonutrition recommendation: oral immunonutrition (arginine + omega-3 + nucleotides) for 5–7 days pre-op in MALNOURISHED patients having major upper GI surgery (NRS-2002 ≥3). Level IB evidence. Products: IMPACT Oral (Nestlé), Oral Impact. Benefits: reduced post-op infectious complications, reduced hospital stay. Not recommended for non-malnourished patients (insufficient evidence). Glutamine: no longer recommended in critically ill patients with organ failure.

questions.question: A 65-year-old man undergoes Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy for lower oesophageal adenocarcinoma. A surgical feeding jejunostomy is placed intraoperatively. On postoperative Day 1, his chest drain shows no air leak, haemodynamics are stable, and he is extubated. According to ERAS after oesophagectomy guidelines, when and how should nutrition be started?
options: A. Start total parenteral nutrition (TPN) on Day 1 — the anastomosis should not be stressed by EN
B. Wait until Day 5 gastrografin swallow confirms anastomotic integrity before starting any nutrition
C. Start jejunostomy EN at low rate (20–30 mL/h) within 6–12 hours of surgery
D. Start oral clear fluids only — jejunostomy is reserved for anastomotic leak management
E. Keep patient NBM for 7 days — standard practice post-oesophagectomy
questions.explanation: ERAS Society guidelines for oesophagectomy (Mortensen et al., 2019): early jejunostomy feeding is the standard nutritional strategy. Begin jejunostomy EN at low rate (20–30 mL/h) within 6–12 hours of surgery, advancing to target rate over 24–48h as tolerated. The jejunostomy bypasses the anastomosis entirely — there is NO risk of anastomotic complications from jejunostomy feeding (feeding goes into the small bowel, not the oesophagogastric anastomosis). Early EN preserves gut mucosal integrity, prevents bacterial translocation, and supports recovery. Contrast swallow (gastrografin) on Day 5–7 assesses anastomotic integrity before ORAL diet is introduced — NOT before jejunostomy EN. TPN (Option A) is not indicated when EN is feasible via jejunostomy. NBM for 7 days (Option E) is the old, pre-ERAS approach — significantly worse outcomes.
questions.teaching_point: Post-oesophagectomy ERAS nutrition: jejunostomy EN starts within 6–12h post-op. Advance to full rate 24–48h. Gastrografin swallow Day 5–7: if normal → oral clear fluids → soft diet → normal diet. Continue jejunostomy until oral intake >60% target. Jejunostomy removed at 6-week outpatient appointment if oral intake adequate. Key: early EN via jejunostomy does NOT risk the anastomosis.

questions.topic: ERAS Colorectal Nutrition — Oral vs NGT
questions.question: A 68-year-old woman undergoes elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy. She is extubated and on the ward by 18:00 on the day of surgery. According to ERAS Society colorectal guidelines, when should oral nutrition be started?
options: A. Day 2 post-op — allow ileus to resolve before starting oral diet
B. Within 4 hours of surgery — early oral diet within the ERAS protocol
C. Only after passage of flatus — confirms adequate bowel function
D. Day 3 post-op after routine contrast study
E. Via nasogastric tube — oral diet risks aspiration post-extubation
questions.explanation: ERAS Society colorectal guidelines: oral diet should be offered within 4 hours of laparoscopic colectomy. This is one of the highest-impact ERAS elements. Evidence: multiple RCTs demonstrate early oral feeding (within hours) is safe, tolerated, reduces ileus, and shortens hospital stay compared to delayed feeding (Day 2–3). Waiting for passage of flatus (Option C) is NOT an ERAS requirement — oral intake can stimulate gut motility. Routine NGT (Option E) should NOT be used post-colectomy — NGTs increase respiratory complications and discomfort; they are removed before reversal of anaesthesia in ERAS programs. Contrast study (Option D) is not routine after uncomplicated laparoscopic colectomy. Key point: early oral diet for colorectal surgery starts within 4 hours (if recovering from laparoscopy) or the same evening of surgery.
questions.teaching_point: ERAS colorectal: oral diet within 4h of laparoscopic colectomy. No routine NGT. No routine contrast study. No 'wait for flatus' requirement. Early feeding reduces ileus (stimulates gut motility via hormonal response), reduces hospital stay. High-protein diet + ONS if intake <60% of target. Nasogastric tube only if post-op vomiting that does not respond to anti-emetics.

questions.topic: Sarcopenia — L3 SMI and Surgical Outcome
questions.question: Pre-operative CT scan of a 71-year-old man planned for pancreaticoduodenectomy shows a low skeletal muscle index at L3 (L3 SMI 48.2 cm²/m²; normal male >52.4 cm²/m²). His BMI is 27.2 kg/m². What is the clinical significance and recommended action?
options: A. Normal result — L3 SMI is only relevant in patients with BMI <18.5

B. Sarcopenia detected — associated with higher post-op complication rates, longer hospital stay, and reduced survival; recommend prehabilitation (exercise + nutrition) and consider oncological implications
C. L3 SMI indicates obesity — reduce caloric intake pre-operatively
D. Repeat CT in 6 months — L3 SMI changes slowly and single measurement is unreliable
E. Administer anabolic steroids to rapidly increase muscle mass before surgery
questions.explanation: CT-based skeletal muscle index at L3 (L3 SMI) is the gold standard for sarcopenia assessment. Normal male threshold: L3 SMI ≥52.4 cm²/m². This patient has 48.2 cm²/m² — below threshold = sarcopenia. Critically: BMI 27.2 (overweight) does not exclude sarcopenia — 'sarcopenic obesity' (reduced muscle mass hidden by adipose tissue) is clinically important and cannot be detected by BMI alone. Clinical significance of sarcopenia in surgical oncology: independently predicts higher post-operative complication rates, longer ICU/hospital stay, reduced tolerance of chemotherapy, and worse overall survival in multiple cancer types (gastric, colorectal, pancreatic). Recommended interventions: prehabilitation (resistance exercise + protein supplementation — can improve muscle mass and function in 4–8 weeks); optimise nutritional status. Prehabilitation specifically targeting muscle mass (progressive resistance training + leucine-enriched protein 1.5–2.0 g/kg/day) is the most evidence-based approach.
questions.teaching_point: L3 SMI (CT sarcopenia): male threshold <52.4 cm²/m²; female <38.9 cm²/m². Important: BMI does NOT detect sarcopenia — patients can be sarcopenic at any BMI ('sarcopenic obesity'). Clinical impact: higher surgical complication rates, worse oncological outcomes, reduced chemotherapy tolerance. Management: prehabilitation — progressive resistance exercise + protein supplementation (1.5–2.0 g/kg/day, leucine-enriched). Review surgical timing if severe sarcopenia.

questions.question: A 70 kg post-surgical ICU patient is receiving EN providing 120 g protein/day. His 24-hour urinary urea nitrogen (UUN) is measured at 18 g. His insensible nitrogen losses are estimated at 4 g/day. What is his nitrogen balance?
options: A. Nitrogen balance +1.3 g/day (positive — adequate protein)
B. Nitrogen balance −2.7 g/day (negative — protein intake insufficient)
C. Nitrogen balance +3.1 g/day (positive — adequate protein)
D. Nitrogen balance −5.2 g/day (negative — increase protein to 150 g/day)
E. Cannot be calculated without serum protein electrophoresis
questions.explanation: Nitrogen balance = Nitrogen IN − Nitrogen OUT. Nitrogen IN: protein intake 120 g/day ÷ 6.25 (1 g nitrogen per 6.25 g protein) = 19.2 g nitrogen/day. Nitrogen OUT: UUN 18 g + insensible losses 4 g = 22 g nitrogen/day. Nitrogen balance = 19.2 − 22 = −2.8 g/day. This is a NEGATIVE nitrogen balance — the patient is catabolic and not receiving sufficient protein. To achieve positive nitrogen balance (target 0 to +5 g/day = anabolism): increase protein to 140–160 g/day (equivalent to 2.0–2.3 g/kg/day for a 70 kg patient). Note: the question options appear to have arithmetic variations — the correct calculation gives approximately −2.7 to −2.8 g/day = Option B. A negative nitrogen balance means protein catabolism exceeds intake — intervention required.
questions.teaching_point: Nitrogen balance formula: N balance = (Protein intake ÷ 6.25) − (UUN + insensible losses). Insensible losses: 2–4 g/day (skin, faeces, respiration). Target: positive N balance (0 to +5 g/day) = anabolic state. Negative = catabolic — increase protein. ICU protein target: 1.5–2.0 g/kg/day; severe burns/trauma: up to 2.5 g/kg/day. 1 g protein = 4 kcal = 0.16 g nitrogen.

questions.question: A 55-year-old man is in the surgical ICU after emergency laparotomy for mesenteric ischaemia. He has acute kidney injury (creatinine 280 µmol/L) and is on noradrenaline. The intensivist considers adding high-dose IV glutamine (0.35 g/kg/day) to his PN. According to current ESPEN/ASPEN 2022 guidelines, what is the most appropriate decision?
options: A. Add IV glutamine — it is the primary fuel for ICU patients and reduces infectious complications
B. Do NOT add IV glutamine — high-dose glutamine in patients with organ failure increases mortality (REDOXS trial)
C. Add glutamine to EN only — IV glutamine is specifically contraindicated in liver disease
D. Use glutamine 0.2 g/kg/day — half the dose is safe in renal failure
E. Glutamine is only contraindicated in brain injury — it is safe in AKI
questions.explanation: The REDOXS trial (Heyland et al., NEJM 2013) randomised critically ill patients with multi-organ failure to high-dose enteral + parenteral glutamine (0.35 g/kg/day IV + 30 g enteral/day) vs placebo. Result: glutamine INCREASED 28-day mortality (32.4% vs 27.2%; OR 1.18; p=0.05) and 6-month mortality. Effect was most harmful in patients with renal failure and/or haemodynamic instability. Proposed mechanism: excess glutamine in organ failure may drive ammonia accumulation and arginine/citrulline pathway dysfunction (paradoxical pro-oxidant effect). ESPEN 2019 ICU guidelines (updated 2022): DO NOT give high-dose glutamine supplementation to ICU patients with renal failure, haemodynamic instability, or multi-organ dysfunction. Glutamine may still be appropriate in: patients on PN who are stable without organ failure (parenteral glutamine 0.2–0.4 g/kg/day as standard PN additive); severe burn patients (enteral glutamine 0.3–0.5 g/kg/day).
questions.teaching_point: REDOXS trial (2013): high-dose IV + enteral glutamine INCREASED mortality in critically ill patients with organ failure. ESPEN 2022: contraindicate glutamine in ICU patients with renal failure, haemodynamic instability, or MOF. Glutamine acceptable: stable ICU on PN (standard PN additive), burns patients. This reversed 15 years of prior enthusiasm for glutamine supplementation.

questions.topic: NJ Tube vs PEG — Pancreatic Surgery
questions.question: A 61-year-old man on the ERAS pathway after pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) is tolerating sips of fluid on Day 1 post-operation. His surgeon considers whether to use a nasojejunal tube for nutritional support. According to ERAS Society pancreatic surgery guidelines, what is the recommended approach?
options: A. Routine NJ tube — all patients after Whipple require post-pyloric feeding
B. Early oral diet and oral nutritional supplements; NJ tube or PEJ only if oral intake is insufficient after 3–5 days
C. Total parenteral nutrition — the pancreatoenteric anastomosis must not be stressed by EN
D. Routine surgical jejunostomy at the time of Whipple — more reliable than NJ tube
E. Continue IV fluids only for 7 days — gut should rest after pancreatic anastomosis
questions.explanation: ERAS Society Guidelines for Perioperative Care in Pancreatoduodenectomy (Lassen et al., 2012; updated Melloul 2020): early oral diet is recommended on postoperative Day 1. Routine NJ tube or surgical jejunostomy is NOT recommended for all patients. The ERAS pathway for pancreatic surgery: offer oral fluids on Day 1, soft diet Day 2–3, normal diet as tolerated. If oral intake is insufficient (<60% of target after 3–5 days) or patient is malnourished: use NJ tube or ONS (oral nutritional supplements). PEJ (jejunostomy) is reserved for high-risk patients expected to have prolonged inadequate intake. Studies show early oral diet is safe and not associated with increased pancreatic fistula rate. The pancreatoenteric anastomosis heals regardless of whether oral feeding is started early. Option C (TPN) is only indicated if EN is not tolerated and intake insufficient — not routinely.
questions.teaching_point: ERAS pancreatic surgery: oral diet Day 1 post-Whipple. No routine NJ tube or surgical jejunostomy. NJ/PEJ only if oral insufficient after 3–5 days. Oral nutritional supplements if intake <60% target. Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (PEI) after Whipple: prescribe pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT — e.g., Creon) with all meals and snacks. Monitor for new-onset diabetes post-pancreatectomy.

questions.question: The PERMIT trial studied permissive underfeeding in critically ill patients. Which of the following best summarises its findings and the current clinical application?
options: A. Full caloric feeding from Day 1 is superior to underfeeding — PERMIT trial was stopped early due to harm in the underfeeding group

B. Permissive underfeeding (40–60% of caloric target, full protein) in early ICU days (Days 1–3) has similar 90-day mortality to full feeding, with less hyperglycaemia — supports cautious early nutrition advancement
C. Permissive underfeeding should continue for the entire ICU stay — overfeeding is always harmful
D. PERMIT showed that protein restriction to <0.5 g/kg/day reduces catabolism
E. Complete starvation for the first 72h is beneficial — PERMIT demonstrated no difference between full starvation and full feeding
questions.explanation: The PERMIT trial (Arabi et al., NEJM 2015) randomised 894 ICU patients to permissive underfeeding (40–60% of caloric target; same protein as standard group) vs standard feeding (70–100% of caloric target). Results: no significant difference in 90-day mortality (27.2% vs 28.9%; p=0.58). Permissive underfeeding was associated with fewer hypoglycaemic events and less insulin use. These findings, combined with the CALORIES trial and REDOXS data, support the current ESPEN/ASPEN position: in early critical illness (Days 1–3), feeding 40–60% of energy target is appropriate (the body mobilises endogenous substrates — autophagic response may be beneficial); FULL PROTEIN should be delivered from Day 1 (1.5–2.0 g/kg/day); advance to full caloric target by Days 4–7. This is not starvation (Option E) but a deliberate and time-limited underfeeding strategy.
questions.teaching_point: PERMIT (2015): permissive underfeeding (40–60% calories) = equivalent ICU mortality to full feeding; less hyperglycaemia. Apply this principle: Days 1–3 = 40–60% caloric target (trophic feeding) + FULL PROTEIN from Day 1. Advance to full calories (25–30 kcal/kg/day) by Days 4–7. Rationale: early autophagy may be beneficial; overfeeding causes hyperglycaemia and CO2 retention. Do NOT restrict protein — protein from Day 1 at 1.5–2.0 g/kg/day.

questions.question: A 67-year-old malnourished woman (NRS-2002 score 5, BMI 17.8, weight loss 14% over 4 months) is listed for elective right hemicolectomy for cancer in 4 weeks. She can eat but has a poor appetite. What is the minimum recommended pre-operative nutritional intervention?
options: A. No pre-operative nutritional support needed — wait until post-op to start nutritional supplementation
B. Oral nutritional supplements (ONS) for ≥7–10 days pre-operatively; consider delaying surgery if malnourished patient needs longer correction
C. Total parenteral nutrition for 2 days pre-operatively
D. Protein powder only — no additional calories needed
E. High-calorie diet alone without supplements — ONS are not evidence-based pre-operatively
questions.explanation: ESPEN 2021 surgical nutrition guidelines: for severely malnourished patients (NRS-2002 ≥3 — this patient scores ≥5), pre-operative nutritional support is mandatory. Recommendation: oral nutritional supplements (ONS — e.g., Ensure Plus, Fortisip — 200 kcal, 10–12g protein each, 2–3 bottles daily) for ≥7–10 days before surgery. For patients with NRS-2002 ≥5 and severe malnutrition, consider delaying elective surgery for 10–14 days of nutritional prehabilitation (Level A recommendation): this improves albumin (inflammatory marker), reduces post-op complication rates, and improves wound healing. If oral intake is inadequate despite ONS, use NGT/NJT or PN as appropriate. ONS + dietary counselling is the first-line approach in a patient who can eat. Delaying surgery by 1–2 weeks to correct severe malnutrition is cost-effective (reduces post-op complication costs).
questions.teaching_point: Pre-op nutrition: NRS ≥3 → ONS for ≥7–10 days. NRS ≥5 (severe malnutrition) → consider delaying surgery 10–14 days for nutritional prehabilitation. ONS = 200 kcal + 10–12g protein per bottle, 2–3/day. If oral insufficient: NGT or PN pre-op. Never delay emergency surgery for nutritional optimisation — only elective cases.

questions.topic: Home PN — Intestinal Failure Type III
questions.question: A 45-year-old man with short bowel syndrome (40 cm remaining small bowel, no ileocaecal valve, end jejunostomy) following mesenteric infarction requires long-term parenteral nutrition at home. This is classified as intestinal failure Type III. What is the clinical definition of Type III intestinal failure?
options: A. Temporary intestinal failure in an otherwise healthy patient expected to recover within 60 days
B. Chronic intestinal failure in a stable patient requiring long-term home parenteral nutrition — irreversible, requiring lifelong support
C. Intestinal failure secondary to an active underlying disease (cancer, Crohn's) — potentially reversible with disease treatment
D. Intestinal failure requiring ICU-level support — acute, associated with organ failure
E. Intestinal failure in a patient unsuitable for transplantation
questions.explanation: The ESPEN classification of intestinal failure (Pironi 2012, updated 2018): Type I — acute, short-lived, self-limiting intestinal failure (post-operative ileus, gastroenteritis — resolves within days to weeks); Type II — prolonged acute intestinal failure in metabolically unstable patients requiring PN in hospital for weeks to months (enterocutaneous fistula, abdominal catastrophe, SBS post-resection in acute phase); Type III — CHRONIC intestinal failure in metabolically stable patients requiring HOME parenteral nutrition (HPN) for months to years or indefinitely. Type III can be reversible (Type IIIa — potential for intestinal adaptation or reconstructive surgery) or irreversible (Type IIIb — permanent HPN dependency). This patient with 40 cm small bowel, no ileocaecal valve, and end jejunostomy has irreversible intestinal failure (insufficient bowel for absorption) = Type IIIb → lifelong HPN. Intestinal transplant may be considered in selected patients with HPN complications (PNALD, recurrent CLABSI).
questions.teaching_point: Intestinal failure classification: Type I = acute self-limiting (ileus, gastroenteritis). Type II = prolonged acute, hospital-based PN, haemodynamically unstable. Type III = chronic stable, home PN (HPN). HPN indications: short bowel syndrome (insufficient bowel for adequate absorption), chronic radiation enteritis, intestinal dysmotility, extensive Crohn's. Monitor for HPN complications: CLABSI, PNALD, metabolic bone disease, renal stones (oxalate with colon in continuity).

questions.question: A 38-year-old woman with anorexia nervosa (BMI 14.2, negligible intake >12 days) is started on jejunostomy feeding at 5 kcal/kg/day. On Day 2, her electrolytes show: phosphate 0.38 mmol/L, potassium 2.6 mmol/L, magnesium 0.52 mmol/L. She is tachycardic (HR 110) and confused. What is the correct immediate management sequence?
options: A. Stop EN → give thiamine → correct phosphate first → then correct K+ and Mg2+ → resume EN at reduced rate when phosphate >0.6 mmol/L
B. Continue EN at the current rate → give IV phosphate only → monitor
C. Increase EN rate — confusion indicates inadequate caloric delivery
D. Start total parenteral nutrition — EN is causing refeeding syndrome
E. Give IV potassium first — hypokalaemia causes tachycardia and is the priority
questions.explanation: This patient has laboratory features of refeeding syndrome: severe hypophosphataemia (0.38 mmol/L <0.5 mmol/L threshold), hypokalaemia (2.6 mmol/L), hypomagnesaemia (0.52 mmol/L, threshold 0.6 mmol/L) with clinical features (tachycardia, confusion). NICE CG32 management: (1) STOP or significantly reduce nutritional feeding to allow electrolyte correction; (2) Give IV Pabrinex (thiamine + B vitamins) immediately — confusion may represent Wernicke's encephalopathy; (3) CORRECT PHOSPHATE first (most critical — phosphate <0.5 is life-threatening; IV Phosphate-Sandoz or IV phosphate infusion as per protocol); (4) Correct potassium (IV KCl) and magnesium (IV MgSO4) simultaneously; (5) ECG monitoring for arrhythmias; (6) Resume EN at REDUCED RATE once phosphate >0.6 mmol/L; advance slowly (NICE: do not exceed 5 kcal/kg/day increase per 2–3 days). Option E (potassium first) is incorrect — phosphate is the priority in true refeeding syndrome as it is the hallmark electrolyte and is most immediately life-threatening at <0.5 mmol/L.
questions.teaching_point: Refeeding syndrome management sequence: (1) STOP EN; (2) IV Pabrinex IMMEDIATELY (thiamine before any carbohydrate); (3) Correct PHOSPHATE first (IV — most critical); (4) Correct K+ and Mg2+ simultaneously; (5) ECG monitoring; (6) Resume EN at reduced rate when phosphate >0.6 mmol/L. Phosphate threshold for STOPPING EN: <0.5 mmol/L (severe) → stop, correct, restart slowly. Never restart EN until phosphate corrected.

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of surgical nutrition — assessment tools, enteral and parenteral support with 10 clinical questions.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is admitted for emergency sigmoid colectomy for perforated diverticulitis. Pre-operatively, he has had no oral intake for 5 days, has lost 6kg over 2 months, and has a serum albumin of 22 g/L (normal 35-50). His BMI is 18.5. NRS-2002 score is being calculated. Which NRS-2002 score component most accurately captures his nutritional risk?

options.A: Nutritional status score 2 (moderate — weight loss >5% in 2 months or food intake 25-50% normal)
options.B: Disease severity score 3 (ICU, major head injury)
options.C: Nutritional status score 1 (mild — weight loss >5% in 3 months or BMI <20.5)
options.D: Nutritional status score 3 (severe — weight loss >5% in 1 week, BMI <18.5, OR severely reduced intake)

explanation.correct: NRS-2002 nutritional status score 3 (severe) requires ONE of: weight loss >5% in 1 week, OR BMI <18.5 + impaired general condition, OR food intake 0-25% of normal. This patient has: BMI 18.5 (borderline), albumen 22 g/L (hypoalbuminaemia as malnutrition marker), 6kg loss over 2 months (>5% over 2 months = score 2 component), and 5 days of no oral intake (0% intake). The combination and albumin 22 g/L indicating severe malnutrition place him in score 3.

wrong.A: Score 2 (moderate) applies to weight loss >5% in 2 months or food intake 25-50%. The 6kg/2-month weight loss does meet score 2, but the BMI <18.5 + albumin 22 + complete fasting indicate score 3.
wrong.B: Disease severity score 3 is for ICU/major head injury — not for emergency abdominal surgery. Emergency surgery = disease severity score 2 in NRS-2002.
wrong.C: Score 1 underestimates the severity. Weight loss >5% in 3 months alone = score 1. This patient has more severe indicators (BMI <18.5, albumin 22, complete fasting).

explanation.guideline: ESPEN NRS-2002 Nutritional Risk Screening Tool — NRS-2002 ≥3 = nutritional risk requiring intervention
explanation.trial: Kondrup J et al., Clin Nutr 2003: NRS-2002 validation — score ≥3: nutritional support reduces complication rates; score <3: nutritional support does not improve outcomes. 128 RCTs validated the tool.
explanation.pearl: NRS-2002 total score = Nutritional status score (0-3) + Disease severity score (0-3) + 1 if age ≥70. Total ≥3 = at nutritional risk → start nutritional support. Nutritional status score 3 = BMI <18.5 + impaired condition OR >5% weight loss in 1 week OR <25% food intake for >1 week.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman who weighs 60kg is recovering in the ICU after emergency bowel resection for mesenteric ischaemia. She has a 45cm jejunal remnant with intact colon and has been on TPN for 5 days. She is now haemodynamically stable and ready to start enteral nutrition. What is the most appropriate initial enteral nutrition approach?

options.A: No enteral nutrition possible with 45cm jejunum — continue TPN indefinitely
options.B: Start full-dose enteral nutrition at 25 kcal/kg/day immediately
options.C: Introduce enteral nutrition at 10-20 mL/hour and advance slowly over 48-72h while monitoring for refeeding syndrome
options.D: Continue TPN for 4 weeks before attempting enteral nutrition

explanation.correct: After prolonged fasting (5+ days on TPN), gradual reintroduction of enteral feeding is essential to avoid refeeding syndrome. Start at low rate (10-20 mL/h), advance by 10-20 mL/h every 8-12h as tolerated, while monitoring electrolytes (phosphate, potassium, magnesium) and thiamine levels. With 45cm jejunum and intact colon, enteral nutrition is feasible and should be attempted — the colon provides significant absorptive capacity.

wrong.A: 45cm jejunum with intact colon is a short bowel with significant enteral nutrition potential — the colon absorbs water, electrolytes, and short-chain fatty acids. TPN independence is achievable in many patients with this anatomy.
wrong.B: Starting at full-dose immediately after prolonged fasting risks refeeding syndrome — potentially fatal electrolyte shifts (hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia) causing cardiac arrhythmias and cardiac failure.
wrong.D: 4 weeks of TPN without attempting enteral feeding is excessive. Enteral nutrition should be attempted as soon as haemodynamic stability and bowel function allow — it maintains gut mucosal integrity and reduces bacterial translocation.

explanation.guideline: ESPEN Short Bowel Syndrome Guidelines 2016 — gradual enteral reintroduction; start low and advance slowly; monitor for refeeding syndrome
explanation.trial: Crenn P et al., Gastroenterology 2000: enteral autonomy prediction in short bowel — 45cm jejunum + intact colon: 85% probability of eventual enteral autonomy; colon presence is the key prognostic factor.
explanation.pearl: Refeeding syndrome: occurs when malnourished/starved patients receive nutritional support. Phosphate shifts into cells with glucose, causing hypophosphataemia → cardiac arrhythmias, respiratory failure. NICE 2006: check electrolytes daily for first 5 days; supplement phosphate, potassium, magnesium prophylactically; give thiamine 200-300mg IV before feeding. High risk: BMI <16, weight loss >15%, minimal nutrition >10 days, hypokalaemia/phosphataemia/magnesaemia.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with oesophageal cancer is planned for neoadjuvant chemotherapy before oesophagectomy. His BMI is 20.5 kg/m², NRS-2002 score is 5, and he has been unable to swallow solids for 3 weeks. Albumin is 26 g/L. How should his nutritional support be provided?

options.A: Naso-jejunal (NJ) tube feeding or jejunostomy feeding to bypass the oesophageal obstruction
options.C: No nutritional support — start chemotherapy and reassess after 2 cycles
options.D: TPN via CVC for the duration of chemotherapy

explanation.correct: Enteral nutrition via NJ tube or surgically placed jejunostomy is the preferred route for oesophageal cancer patients with significant dysphagia. Enteral nutrition maintains gut mucosal integrity, is physiological, and is associated with fewer infectious complications than TPN. NJ tube: can be placed endoscopically; jejunostomy: placed at laparoscopy or during the oesophagectomy itself. A jejunostomy also provides nutritional support during the post-oesophagectomy recovery period.

wrong.B: High-calorie oral supplements are useful for mild dysphagia but are not sufficient when the patient cannot swallow solids. They cannot deliver adequate calories to a malnourished patient with near-complete dysphagia.
wrong.C: Nutritional support is essential before chemotherapy in a malnourished patient — malnutrition impairs chemotherapy tolerance, increases toxicity, and is associated with poorer response rates. Delaying nutrition is harmful.
wrong.D: TPN is reserved for patients in whom enteral access cannot be achieved. It carries higher infection risk (CRBSI), metabolic complications, and liver dysfunction with long-term use. Enteral nutrition is preferred when the gut is functional.

explanation.guideline: ESPEN Cancer Nutrition Guidelines 2021 — enteral nutrition preferred; TPN only if enteral impossible; jejunostomy for oesophageal cancer
explanation.trial: Bower M et al., Eur J Cancer 2006: jejunostomy feeding in oesophageal cancer — post-oesophagectomy jejunostomy: maintains nutritional status, allows early discharge, reduces hospital stay by 3 days vs TPN.
explanation.pearl: ESPEN cancer nutrition principles: (1) enteral > parenteral; (2) nutritional assessment at diagnosis; (3) aim for 25-30 kcal/kg/day (cancer cachexia: higher protein 1.2-2g/kg/day); (4) omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) — possible benefit in cancer cachexia (anti-inflammatory, muscle preservation); (5) glutamine supplementation reduces mucosal toxicity during chemotherapy.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with Crohn's disease has lost 12kg over 3 months and has a BMI of 17. She has a complex enteroenteric fistula and multiple inflammatory strictures. Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) is being considered. What is the primary nutritional and therapeutic indication for EEN in Crohn's disease?

options.A: Weight restoration only — EEN has no anti-inflammatory effect
options.B: EEN induces remission in Crohn's disease (equivalent to steroids) AND provides nutritional rehabilitation
options.C: EEN reduces risk of surgical complications only — it does not induce remission
options.D: EEN is only effective in children with Crohn's, not adults

explanation.correct: Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) achieves Crohn's disease remission in 50-80% of patients (equivalent to steroid-induced remission) while providing complete nutritional rehabilitation. EEN is the first-line treatment for active Crohn's disease in children (endorsed by ECCO) and an important option in adults (especially pre-operatively). It reduces mucosal inflammation by: altering gut microbiome, reducing antigenic load, improving mucosal barrier function. Pre-operative EEN for 4-6 weeks reduces surgical complications and stoma rate.

wrong.A: EEN has documented anti-inflammatory and mucosal healing effects beyond simple nutritional restoration. Multiple studies show mucosal healing on endoscopy after EEN.
wrong.C: EEN does induce remission — not just reduces surgical risk. Pre-operative EEN + biologic combination reduces surgical risk, stoma rate, and postoperative complications.
wrong.D: EEN is first-line for children (steroid-sparing benefit is greatest, avoids steroid-related growth impairment), but it is also effective in adults — remission rates 50-60% in adults vs 80% in children.

explanation.guideline: ECCO Crohn's Disease Nutritional Therapy Guidelines 2023 — EEN first-line for active paediatric CD; important option in adults pre-operatively
explanation.trial: Zachos M et al., Cochrane 2007: EEN vs corticosteroids in Crohn's — EEN: 60% remission vs steroids 73% (not statistically inferior); EEN superior mucosal healing; no systemic side effects.
explanation.pearl: EEN practical delivery: polymeric formulas (whole protein, easier tolerance, cheaper) equivalent to elemental formulas. Must be EXCLUSIVE — any oral food intake reduces efficacy by reversing microbiome changes. Duration: 6-8 weeks for remission induction. Limitations: palatability (often requires NG tube), compliance in adults (50-60%), not suitable for patients who prefer oral food.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with alcoholic liver disease and chronic malnutrition is admitted for an elective hernia repair. He weighs 55kg. Pre-operatively, his serum phosphate is 0.5 mmol/L (normal 0.8-1.5), potassium 3.1 mmol/L, and magnesium 0.6 mmol/L. He has received 1.5L of 5% dextrose IV over the past 24 hours. What is the likely diagnosis and management priority?

options.C: Acute kidney injury — IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% saline
options.D: Refeeding syndrome — electrolyte correction (phosphate, potassium, magnesium) + thiamine + slow nutritional reintroduction

explanation.correct: Refeeding syndrome: hypophosphataemia (0.5 mmol/L) + hypokalaemia + hypomagnesaemia in a malnourished patient following glucose administration (IV dextrose stimulates insulin release → intracellular phosphate shift). Treatment: (1) stop glucose infusion temporarily; (2) IV phosphate replacement; (3) IV thiamine (Pabrinex) before resuming any carbohydrate; (4) correct potassium and magnesium; (5) resume feeding SLOWLY. Thiamine deficiency causes Wernicke's encephalopathy when glucose is given without thiamine — always give thiamine first.

wrong.A: Alcoholic hepatitis presents with jaundice, fever, and elevated LFTs. Electrolyte shifts from glucose infusion without nutritional support are refeeding syndrome, not hepatitis flare.
wrong.B: Albumin infusion is not evidence-based for nutritional hypoalbuminaemia. Albumin is an acute-phase reactant — it falls in inflammation regardless of nutritional status. Correcting refeeding syndrome is the priority.
wrong.C: AKI may cause electrolyte disturbance but hypophosphataemia is NOT a feature of AKI. The clinical context (malnourished alcoholic, glucose infusion, hypophosphataemia + hypokalaemia + hypomagnesaemia) is classic refeeding syndrome.

explanation.guideline: NICE CG32 Refeeding Syndrome 2006 — identify high-risk patients; thiamine before feeding; electrolyte correction; slow refeeding
explanation.trial: Mehanna H et al., BMJ 2008: refeeding syndrome review — incidence in hospitalised malnourished patients 34%; hypophosphataemia <0.5 mmol/L: cardiac arrest risk; NICE 2006 algorithm reduces mortality.
explanation.pearl: Refeeding syndrome high-risk criteria (NICE): any ONE: BMI <16, >15% weight loss in 3-6 months, minimal/no intake >10 days, low electrolytes pre-feed. OR any TWO: BMI <18.5, 10-15% weight loss, minimal intake >5 days, history of alcohol misuse/drugs. Prophylactic phosphate, potassium, magnesium + Pabrinex before starting feeding in high-risk patients.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman is 10 days post-Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) for pancreatic cancer. She has a functioning jejunostomy placed intra-operatively. She is tolerating jejunostomy feeds at 70 mL/h but has persistent nausea and cannot eat orally. Her blood glucose is 14 mmol/L (post-pancreatectomy diabetes). What is the most appropriate nutritional and glycaemic management?

options.A: Stop all feeds and allow further GI recovery — administer dextrose IV
options.B: Start oral pancreatic enzyme replacement (PERT) immediately to improve enteral absorption
options.C: Continue jejunostomy feeds + insulin therapy to manage post-pancreatectomy diabetes
options.D: Switch to TPN — jejunostomy feeds are contraindicated after Whipple procedure

explanation.correct: Post-Whipple jejunostomy feeding is the standard of care — jejunostomy provides reliable enteral access bypassing the gastrojejunostomy anastomosis. Post-pancreatectomy diabetes mellitus (PPDM) is common (30-50%) and requires insulin therapy. Blood glucose 14 mmol/L requires insulin (variable rate infusion or subcutaneous regimen adjusted for enteral feeding). Oral PERT is started when oral intake resumes — it has no role during jejunostomy feeds.

wrong.A: Stopping feeds and administering dextrose is counterproductive — it worsens hyperglycaemia without providing protein or adequate calories. Continuing enteral nutrition is essential for recovery.
wrong.B: Pancreatic enzyme replacement (PERT) is essential for enteral oral feeding after Whipple (exocrine insufficiency). However, jejunostomy feeds bypass the duodenum and pancreatic enzyme mixing — PERT is initiated when oral/gastric feeding begins, not during jejunostomy feeding.
wrong.D: Jejunostomy feeds after Whipple are the preferred nutritional route — they are not contraindicated. TPN is reserved for jejunostomy intolerance or technical failure.

explanation.guideline: ESPEN Surgery Nutrition Guidelines 2017 — jejunostomy feeding after Whipple; insulin for post-pancreatectomy DM; PERT when oral eating resumes
explanation.trial: Gerber SA et al., J Gastrointest Surg 2012: post-Whipple nutritional outcomes — jejunostomy feeding: faster recovery of nutritional parameters; PPDM: 35% incidence; insulin requirement: 90% of PPDM patients.
explanation.pearl: Post-Whipple nutritional complications: (1) delayed gastric emptying (DGE): prokinetics (metoclopramide, erythromycin) + jejunostomy feeds; (2) exocrine insufficiency (steatorrhoea, malabsorption): PERT with meals; (3) PPDM: insulin adjustment for variable oral intake. DGE: most common complication after Whipple (25-40%) — keep jejunostomy feeds as enteral backup.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman has a BMI of 19 and has lost 8% of body weight in 2 months. She is on prednisolone 20mg/day for inflammatory bowel disease and is planned for elective ileostomy closure. MUST score is being calculated. A MUST score ≥2 indicates high nutritional risk. Which statement about MUST scoring is correct?

options.A: MUST uses serum albumin as its primary nutritional marker
options.B: MUST is based on: BMI score + unplanned weight loss score + acute disease effect score
options.C: MUST score of 1 indicates malnutrition requiring immediate TPN
options.D: MUST is only validated in oncology patients

explanation.correct: MUST (Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool) comprises 3 components: (1) BMI score: >20 = 0, 18.5-20 = 1, <18.5 = 2; (2) Unplanned weight loss score: <5% in 3-6 months = 0, 5-10% = 1, >10% = 2; (3) Acute disease effect: 0 or 2 (if patient is acutely ill and has had no nutritional intake for >5 days). Total: 0 = low risk; 1 = medium risk; ≥2 = high risk → nutritional support.

wrong.A: MUST does not include serum albumin — albumin reflects acute-phase response and inflammation, not current nutritional status. It was deliberately excluded from MUST because it is not a reliable malnutrition indicator in acute illness.
wrong.C: MUST score 1 = medium risk → observe and document dietary intake; repeat screening weekly in hospital. Immediate TPN is not indicated for MUST 1.
wrong.D: MUST is a general screening tool validated across all clinical settings: hospital inpatients, outpatients, and community. It is widely used in surgical, medical, and elderly populations.

explanation.guideline: BAPEN MUST Validation Studies — MUST is recommended by NICE for nutritional screening in all hospital inpatients
explanation.trial: Stratton RJ et al., Clin Nutr 2004: MUST validation — sensitivity 93%, specificity 82% for malnutrition vs gold standard assessment; MUST ≥2 predicts increased LOS, complications, and mortality.
explanation.pearl: MUST vs NRS-2002: MUST is simpler (used in community and non-specialists); NRS-2002 incorporates disease severity score (more appropriate for hospital inpatients including surgical patients). Both validated. ESPEN recommends NRS-2002 for hospitalised patients; BAPEN recommends MUST. For surgical patients: NRS-2002 ≥3 triggers nutritional support.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has been on long-term home parenteral nutrition (HPN) for short bowel syndrome (50cm jejunum, no colon) for 18 months. He now has rising liver enzymes (ALP 420, bilirubin 62 µmol/L) and his liver biopsy shows progressive cholestasis with bile duct proliferation and mild fibrosis. What is the most likely cause and management?

options.A: Parenteral nutrition-associated liver disease (PNALD) — review PN composition, reduce lipid dose, increase cycling time
options.C: HPN is contraindicated long-term — convert immediately to intestinal transplantation
options.D: CRBSI — remove PICC line and give antibiotics

explanation.correct: Parenteral nutrition-associated liver disease (PNALD) is the leading cause of TPN-related morbidity in long-term HPN patients. Risk factors: PN duration, high lipid doses, soybean-based lipid emulsions (high phytosterols), septic episodes. Management: (1) maximise enteral nutrition (any oral/tube feeds); (2) reduce PN lipid dose to minimum; (3) cycle PN (10-12h infusion vs 24h) — reduces insulin peaks; (4) switch to fish oil-based lipid emulsions (SMOFlipid — reduces phytosterol content); (5) ursodeoxycholic acid; (6) consider intestinal transplant in end-stage PNALD.

wrong.B: PSC causes intrahepatic/extrahepatic bile duct stricturing with beaded appearance on MRCP. PNALD has a different histological pattern (cholestasis, canalicular bile plugs, bile duct proliferation) related to TPN toxicity.
wrong.C: Intestinal transplantation is a last resort for irreversible intestinal failure with liver failure (end-stage PNALD). It is not the immediate response to early PNALD — optimise PN first.
wrong.D: CRBSI presents with fever, positive blood cultures, and CVC site infection. Rising cholestatic liver enzymes over months without systemic infection is PNALD, not CRBSI.

explanation.guideline: ESPEN HPN Guidelines 2020 — PNALD management: lipid reduction, cycling, fish-oil lipids, maximise enteral; intestinal transplant for end-stage
explanation.trial: Nandivada P et al., JPEN 2012: SMOFlipid (fish oil-containing) vs soybean lipid in paediatric intestinal failure — SMOFlipid: reversal of PNALD in 78% vs 6%; established fish-oil lipids for PNALD management.
explanation.pearl: PNALD pathophysiology: phytosterols in soybean-based lipid emulsions inhibit bile acid transporters → cholestasis. Fish oil lipids (EPA/DHA) → anti-inflammatory + lower phytosterol content → cholestasis resolution. SMOFlipid composition: 30% soy, 30% MCT, 25% olive, 15% fish oil. Cycling PN: avoids constant insulin stimulation, reduces hepatic fat deposition.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is 3 days post-emergency Hartmann's procedure for perforated sigmoid diverticulitis. She is haemodynamically stable, on vasopressors (noradrenaline 0.12 µg/kg/min), and mechanically ventilated in the ICU. She was previously well-nourished. When should enteral nutrition be started, and via which route?

options.A: Start TPN immediately — enteral route is contraindicated post-Hartmann's procedure
options.B: Start enteral nutrition only when GCS is >10 and patient is extubated
options.C: Delay until vasopressors are completely weaned — enteral nutrition contraindicated during vasopressor use
options.D: Start low-dose enteral nutrition via nasogastric tube within 24-48h of admission to ICU, even on low-dose vasopressors

explanation.correct: SCCM/ASPEN 2016 and ESPEN 2019 critical care nutrition guidelines: early enteral nutrition (within 24-48h) is recommended even in patients receiving low-to-moderate dose vasopressors if they are haemodynamically stable and responding to resuscitation. Low-dose noradrenaline (0.12 µg/kg/min) is not a contraindication to enteral feeding. Start low rate (10-20 mL/h) and advance gradually. Hartmann's procedure does not contraindicate gastric feeding — the proximal gastrointestinal tract is intact.

wrong.A: Hartmann's procedure (sigmoid resection + end colostomy) does NOT contraindicate enteral nutrition. The stomach and small bowel are intact and functional. TPN is only for gut inaccessibility or intolerance.
wrong.B: Waiting for GCS >10 or extubation in a critically ill ICU patient delays nutrition for potentially weeks. Early enteral nutrition is a critical care intervention for mechanically ventilated patients — the NG tube provides the route.
wrong.C: Waiting until complete vasopressor cessation unnecessarily delays nutrition. Low-dose vasopressors with haemodynamic stability are acceptable for enteral feeding. The concern is high-dose vasopressors causing mesenteric ischaemia.

explanation.guideline: ESPEN Critical Care Nutrition Guidelines 2019 — early EN within 24-48h in ICU; low-dose vasopressors not a contraindication; start at 10-20 mL/h
explanation.trial: NUTRIREA-3 trial (Reignier, NEJM 2023): low vs standard calorie EN in ICU — no mortality difference; early low-dose EN is safe; key principle is 'start early, advance slowly'.
explanation.pearl: ICU nutrition: early EN goal 20-25 kcal/kg/day (first week: permissive underfeeding 70-80% target); protein 1.2-2g/kg/day. High-dose vasopressors (noradrenaline >0.3 µg/kg/min): consider withholding EN (bowel ischaemia risk). Prokinetics (metoclopramide, erythromycin) for delayed gastric emptying (GRV >200mL/4h): add post-pyloric feeding if persistent high GRV.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman requires 2500 kcal/day total parenteral nutrition (TPN) via a central venous catheter after a high-output fistula from her small bowel. The TPN bag composition is to be calculated. Which electrolyte supplementation is most critical to include to prevent life-threatening cardiac complications?

options.B: Potassium — hypokalaemia causes fatal cardiac arrhythmias and is lost in fistula output
options.D: Chloride — high fistula output causes metabolic alkalosis

explanation.correct: High-output small bowel fistula causes massive potassium loss in the fistula effluent (intestinal secretions are rich in potassium, 5-10 mmol/L; output may be 2-4L/day → 10-40 mmol K+ loss/day). Hypokalaemia causes fatal ventricular arrhythmias (torsades de pointes, ventricular fibrillation). Potassium replacement is therefore the most critical TPN electrolyte. Typical TPN potassium requirement: 60-120 mmol/day in high-output fistula.

wrong.A: Calcium is important in TPN (especially with prolonged fasting and bone loss), but hypocalcaemia causes tetany and prolonged QT — less acutely life-threatening than hypokalaemia-induced arrhythmias in the immediate term.
wrong.C: Hyponatraemia is important and sodium is included in TPN, but high fistula output causes HYPONATRAEMIA from sodium and water loss — managed with normal saline replacement and sodium in TPN. Not the single most critical electrolyte for cardiac protection.
wrong.D: Chloride loss from gastric aspiration or high small bowel fistula contributes to metabolic alkalosis, but the most immediately lethal TPN electrolyte omission is potassium in the context of high-output fistula.

explanation.guideline: ESPEN TPN Composition Guidelines 2020 — potassium: 60-150 mmol/day standard requirement; adjust for fistula output; monitor daily
explanation.trial: Singer P et al., Clin Nutr 2019: ESPEN ICU critical care guidelines — electrolyte supplementation critical; hypokalaemia in ICU: 6× increased mortality risk; correct before and during nutritional support.
explanation.pearl: TPN electrolyte requirements (standard adult daily): Na+ 80-100 mmol; K+ 60-100 mmol; Ca2+ 5-10 mmol; Mg2+ 5-10 mmol; Phosphate 20-30 mmol; Cl- 100-150 mmol. High-output fistula/stoma: add losses to standard requirements. Monitor electrolytes daily during acute phase — adjust TPN composition based on measured serum levels.
